metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the fence seeding and increment rule to the blob schema spec: e.g. 'fence starts at 1 on first acquire and increments by 1 on each successful steal-CAS (C2); a plain acquire on a free ref always writes fence=1.'
findings.finding: The `fence` field is described as 'a monotonic int (progress witness + fencing token)' but no seeding or increment rule is given: is fence=0 on first acquire? Is it read from the existing blob and incremented by 1? Derived from a hash? An executor implementing acquire will make an arbitrary choice.
findings.location: What section, 'Blob = newline-terminated UTF-8 JSON {holder, lease_secs, heartbeat_ns, fence}'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `fence` is initialized on each acquire to ensure strict monotonicity across lock cycles (e.g. derive from `time.time_ns()`, read the last fence from the just-deleted blob before release, or maintain a separate persistent fence counter ref); document this in the ADR.
findings.finding: The `fence` field is described as a 'monotonic int (progress witness + fencing token)' but no mechanism is specified to ensure monotonicity across lock cycles. If the ref is deleted on release and re-created on the next acquire, a new holder could initialize fence=1, resetting the counter — invalidating any fencing token checks made by consumers who observed a higher fence value in a previous lock cycle.
findings.location: What section — 'Blob = newline-terminated UTF-8 JSON {holder, lease_secs, heartbeat_ns, fence}'
findings.suggested_fix: Simplest approach: derive fence from `time.time_ns()` at acquire time (same clock already used for heartbeat_ns). Since time_ns is monotonically non-decreasing across acquires separated by any real-world interval, this provides a practical fencing token without a separate counter ref. Document this choice in the ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `fence` field semantics in the blob schema: its initial value on `acquire` (e.g. 0 or 1), who increments it (acquire only, or also renew), the increment step, and whether the value is sourced from a counter persisted outside the blob or derived from the blob itself.
findings.finding: The blob schema field `fence` is described as 'a monotonic int (progress witness + fencing token)' but its initialization value, increment rule (who increments it, on which operation, by how much), and placement (acquire only? renew?) are never specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: What section — Blob schema definition: '{holder, lease_secs, heartbeat_ns, fence}'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the blob schema section: 'fence is initialized to 0 on the first acquire of a free ref (old=zeros CAS). It is not incremented by renew() in C1; C2 steal-CAS will increment it on steal. The fence value is stored in the blob itself — no external counter.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify concretely how acquire bypasses `_cas_advance_with_retry`'s retry loop for exit-128 (e.g., call the acquire callable directly without the retry wrapper, or add a `retry_on_mismatch=False` flag to the helper).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `_cas_advance_with_retry` can be reused for the acquire path with 'NOT retried' semantics for exit-128, but the existing function unconditionally retries up to `_CAS_RETRY_BUDGET` on any `_is_cas_mismatch` exit-128, including the create-only acquire case. No mechanism is specified to make the acquire invocation non-retrying while releasing continues to use the retry budget.
findings.location: Plan section 'Acquire vs release retry semantics differ'; AC line 'acquire exit-128 ("held") is NOT retried while release exit-128 is treated as idempotent success'
findings.suggested_fix: For acquire: call the `git hash-object` + `git update-ref` sequence directly (no retry wrapper), catching exit-128 and translating it to 'lock held'. Reserve `_cas_advance_with_retry` for release (where exit-128 is treated as idempotent success and the retry budget applies to genuine concurrent races).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `_cas_advance_with_retry` is adapted for per-operation retry policy: e.g., add a `on_cas_mismatch` callback (returning `raise | succeed | retry`) or extract a lower-level `_run_update_ref_cas` that each operation wraps with its own policy, so acquire (stop), release (succeed), and steal (stop) each get the correct outcome without ambiguity.
findings.finding: `_cas_advance_with_retry` has a single, hardwired retry policy (retry on `_is_cas_mismatch`, up to `_CAS_RETRY_BUDGET`). The plan requires three distinct exit-128 outcomes: acquire = stop immediately (lock already held, not retried); release = treat as idempotent success; steal-CAS (C2) = loser stops. None of these three can be expressed through the current helper without modifying its loop — yet the plan mandates reuse of the helper without specifying the plumbing (policy callback, wrapper try/except, or a new thin variant). An implementer following the plan as written will either misapply retry semantics (silently retrying an acquire that should stop) or be forced to diverge from the 'reuse' directive.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Acquire vs release retry semantics differ' section; AC line 'The CAS reuses `_cas_advance_with_retry` … acquire exit-128 ("held") is NOT retried while release exit-128 is treated as idempotent success'
findings.suggested_fix: Extract a new `_run_update_ref_cas(repo_root, ref, new_oid, old_oid) -> Literal['ok','mismatch']` that performs the single `git update-ref` call and returns a discriminated result. Each operation (`acquire`, `release`) implements its own policy on top: acquire raises on `'mismatch'`; release returns success on `'mismatch'`. Reserve `_cas_advance_with_retry` (with its retry loop) for the existing tickets-branch callers only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the module's API and blob-vs-commit branching outcome upfront: either (a) define the `_ref_lock.py` implementation for the commit-based fallback (schema, acquire/release/read contract) so it is executable without clarification, or (b) explicitly make AC0 a standalone spike whose outcome gates a separate task for the module implementation — not the same task.
findings.finding: If AC0's feasibility spike finds that GitHub rejects blob refs and the fallback (ref → tiny-commit) is triggered, all subsequent ACs that assume blob semantics (AC1: 'blob is created via git hash-object -w', AC2: blob is UTF-8 JSON decoded from a git object) become invalid — yet no alternative ACs or module design is specified for the commit path. The executor must redesign the module mid-session without guidance.
findings.location: AC0 / What section 'If GitHub rejects it, fall back to ref -> tiny-commit'
findings.suggested_fix: Split into two tasks: Task C1a (AC0 spike only — runs in CI, documents working refspec in ADR, produces a go/no-go decision) and Task C1b (module implementation, whose ACs are written after the spike result is known). This removes the ambiguous 'if rejected, fall back and redesign' branch from the executor's in-session scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state that AC1-AC5 are contingent on AC0's blob path succeeding, and add either (a) a note that the plan will be revised post-spike for the fallback path, or (b) parallel fallback ACs covering the commit-object schema, so the plan has a valid done-state in both branches.
findings.finding: AC0's fallback ('ref -> tiny-commit') contradicts the implementation ACs (AC1, AC2) which are fully specified for the blob path only; if the fallback is triggered, the module spec and blob-schema ACs become incorrect, yet the plan has no revision mechanism or fallback-path ACs.
findings.location: AC0: 'If GitHub rejects it, fall back to ref -> tiny-commit and record that in the ADR.' vs. AC1: 'blob is created via git hash-object -w' and AC2: 'Blob is UTF-8 JSON {holder, lease_secs, heartbeat_ns, fence}'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence after AC0: 'If the fallback path is taken, ACs 1-2 will be revised to cover the commit-object schema before implementation begins; this plan documents the blob-path target only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `release` uses `git update-ref -d <ref> <old-oid>` (deletion CAS) so the ref is fully absent after release, allowing subsequent create-only acquire (old=zeros) to succeed; document this in the ADR and AC.
findings.finding: The release path does not explicitly specify that the ref is DELETED (git update-ref -d <ref> <old-oid>). If release merely updates or leaves the ref pointing at a different blob OID, then subsequent create-only acquire (old=40 zeros) will fail unconditionally because the ref still exists — breaking the acquire-on-free-ref contract and leaking ref namespace.
findings.location: What section — 'Blob creation' / 'Acquire vs release retry semantics differ' / AC line 'acquire is create-only CAS (old=zeros), release is observed-oid CAS'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'release is observed-oid CAS' with 'release is `git update-ref -d <ref> <old-oid>` (delete-if-matches CAS)'. A release exit-128 (already deleted) remains idempotent success. This makes the ref lifecycle: absent → present (acquire) → absent (release), preserving the create-only acquire invariant across lock cycles.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve and unify the stale-OID release contract in both the What section and the AC: decide whether a CAS exit-128 on release is (a) silently treated as idempotent success or (b) raises a named error, then state that single answer in both places so the unit test has an unambiguous observable outcome to assert.
findings.finding: The plan contradicts itself on stale-OID release semantics: the What section says 'A release CAS exit-128 is a benign race (already released) and is treated as success (idempotent release),' but the AC unit-test line says 'a stale-oid release is rejected' — leaving the implementer to choose between raising and returning success, with the test pinning whichever interpretation happens to be coded first.
findings.location: AC line: 'release requires the correct old oid and a stale-oid release is rejected' vs. What section: 'A release CAS exit-128 is … treated as success (idempotent release)'
findings.suggested_fix: Choose: if the design goal is idempotent release (safer for the reconciler), update the AC to 'a stale-oid release is treated as success (idempotent); the test asserts no exception is raised and the call returns normally.' If rejection is intended (e.g., to surface bugs where the wrong holder calls release), update the What section and add a named exception class to the module's public contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exception class that `read()` raises on corrupt/partial/empty blob decode (e.g. `RefLockBlobError`), specify which module owns it, and declare it in the AC so callers can import and catch the correct type.
findings.finding: The plan states `read` of a corrupt/partial/empty blob 'raises a defined error' but never names or defines that error class — the error type's vocabulary (class name, module, inherits from what) is absent from the plan and does not exist in the codebase.
findings.location: What section — 'Decode of a corrupt/partial/empty blob fails closed: read raises a defined error and callers treat "cannot parse" as HELD'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the blob schema section: 'read() raises RefLockBlobError (defined in _ref_lock.py, subclasses ValueError) on any decode failure; callers must catch RefLockBlobError and treat the lock as HELD.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC4 a unit test that exercises `_is_cas_mismatch` (or its successor) with a simulated exit-128 stderr for `refs/reconciler/lock` and asserts it is classified as a CAS mismatch (not a generic error), proving the ref-name generalization works.
findings.finding: No unit test or proving command is specified to verify that the generalized `_is_cas_mismatch` correctly classifies a CAS exit-128 on `refs/reconciler/*` (as opposed to the formerly hard-coded `refs/heads/tickets`). The AC covers acquire/release/read behaviors but not the discriminator's ref-name generalization.
findings.location: AC3 / What section '_is_cas_mismatch (`:415`) is generalized off the hard-coded refs/heads/tickets to accept the ref name'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a timeout for git push/fetch operations used in acquire/release (e.g. `git -c http.lowSpeedLimit=0 -c http.lowSpeedTime=30 push …`), specify the error classification for remote-I/O failures (treat as 'cannot acquire' / raise a typed exception), and document whether the reconciler degrades gracefully when the lock remote is unavailable.
findings.finding: No timeout is specified for the remote git push/fetch operations that back every lock acquire and release. If the GitHub remote is unreachable, slow, or returns an error (network partition, token expiry, GitHub outage), the acquire/release call can block indefinitely or raise an unclassified exception — with no stated failure mode, circuit-breaker, or degraded behaviour.
findings.location: AC0 / What section 'AC0 feasibility spike' / AC line 'acquire is create-only CAS … release is observed-oid CAS'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `fence` and `heartbeat_ns` are intentionally pre-defined in the C1 schema to lock in the C2 contract, or whether they should be deferred to C2 — if pre-defined, add at least one AC asserting their initial values (e.g., `fence=0` on first acquire, `heartbeat_ns=acquisition_time`) so the schema is tested rather than write-only.
findings.finding: `fence` and `heartbeat_ns` blob fields have no C1 AC consumer — they are pre-designed for C2 (steal-CAS / lease-expiry) with YAGNI risk.
findings.location: What section — Blob schema definition: `{holder, lease_secs, heartbeat_ns, fence}`; also AC line: 'Blob is UTF-8 JSON `{holder, lease_secs, heartbeat_ns, fence}`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the unit-test AC: 'acquire sets fence=0 (or fence=1) and heartbeat_ns=acquisition_time_ns' so the initial values are specified and tested in C1 rather than left as unexercised metadata.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the ref name is threaded from `_cas_advance_with_retry` into its internal `_is_cas_mismatch` call (e.g., add a `ref_name` parameter to `_cas_advance_with_retry`, or extract a ref-agnostic variant that inspects `update-ref` in args without checking the specific ref).
findings.finding: `_cas_advance_with_retry` calls `_is_cas_mismatch(exc)` directly inside its retry loop (line 469), so generalizing `_is_cas_mismatch` to accept a ref-name parameter requires also changing the call-site inside `_cas_advance_with_retry`. The plan specifies generalizing `_is_cas_mismatch` but does not address threading the ref name into the retry loop that calls it — leaving the wiring ambiguous.
findings.location: Plan section 'Reuse the real CAS helper'; AC line 'The CAS reuses `_cas_advance_with_retry` … `_is_cas_mismatch` (`:415`) is generalized'
findings.suggested_fix: Extract a ref-agnostic `_is_update_ref_cas_mismatch(exc)` that checks `'update-ref' in args and exc.returncode == 128` without constraining the ref name; use it inside `_cas_advance_with_retry` and keep the old `_is_cas_mismatch` as a wrapper for backward compatibility.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate (or rule out) the required co-edit at `_advisory_lock.py:469` — if `_is_cas_mismatch` gains a ref-name parameter, document that `_cas_advance_with_retry`'s internal call at line 469 must be updated to pass the ref name; if the ref-agnostic variant is chosen instead, state that explicitly so the call site is unaffected.
findings.finding: `_cas_advance_with_retry` calls `_is_cas_mismatch(exc)` at line 469 with no ref-name argument. The plan says `_is_cas_mismatch` will be 'generalized to accept the ref name,' but does not enumerate the line-469 call site in `_cas_advance_with_retry` as a required co-edit. If the signature of `_is_cas_mismatch` grows a ref-name parameter, this internal call breaks at runtime.
findings.location: `_advisory_lock.py` line 469 / plan section 'Reuse the real CAS helper'
findings.suggested_fix: Commit to the ref-agnostic variant (check only `'update-ref' in args and exc.returncode == 128`, dropping the `refs/heads/tickets` guard) — this generalizes `_is_cas_mismatch` without changing its signature, leaving the line-469 call site untouched and preserving all existing test coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC4) to verify that structured log events are emitted on acquire-failure (lock held) and stale-oid release, so this described deliverable has a covering criterion.
findings.finding: The body describes 'structured log emission for acquire-failure (already held) and release on a stale oid' as a deliverable, but no AC covers it — it is an orphan deliverable with no verifiable acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Add structured log emission for acquire-failure (already held) and release on a stale oid.' / Acceptance Criteria section (no matching AC).
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC4 to include: 'structured log events are emitted on acquire-failure (lock already held) and on release with a stale OID.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before promoting `_cas_advance_with_retry` to a semipublic symbol, confirm the promotion scope: if the intent is simply to make it importable from `_ref_lock.py` within the same package (module-internal reuse, not public API), document that in the promoted symbol's docstring and note the retry-semantics asymmetry (acquire callers must pass a `mutate_and_advance` that raises without retrying on 'already held').
findings.finding: `_cas_advance_with_retry` currently has only 1 call-site in the codebase; promoting it to a semipublic shared symbol adds a second (from `_ref_lock.py`), which is still below the Rule-of-Three threshold for a formally promoted shared API.
findings.location: What section — 'Reuse the real CAS helper `_cas_advance_with_retry` … Promote the reused private helper to a shared/semipublic symbol as needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC0 must explicitly confirm (and document in the ADR) that `contents:write` alone is sufficient for pushing `refs/reconciler/*` to the GitHub remote — if a broader grant is needed, the ADR must justify the minimum scope and note any ruleset bypass implications (least-privilege, T5c).
findings.finding: The plan designates `contents:write` as the required GITHUB_TOKEN permission for pushing `refs/reconciler/*` blobs, but does not verify that this standard branch-write grant is sufficient for arbitrary non-branch ref namespaces — GitHub's ruleset and ref-permission model may require additional grants (e.g., a fine-grained PAT or `administration:write`) for custom ref namespaces that don't match `refs/heads/*` or `refs/tags/*`, which would mean the plan under-scopes the credential.
findings.location: AC0 / 'What' section — 'CI `GITHUB_TOKEN` (contents:write)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC0 spike test, capture the exact GitHub token permission scope used and record in the ADR whether `contents:write` sufficed or whether a different/broader scope was required; if broader, document why it is still the minimum necessary and add a note that repo rulesets must not be widened to cover refs/reconciler/* without revisiting this grant.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the expected maximum concurrency (number of simultaneous acquirers) and acquire/release call frequency; confirm that `_cas_advance_with_retry`'s retry count and backoff are appropriate for remote git I/O (or cap/tune them for the reconciler-ref case).
findings.finding: No load or concurrency expectations are stated for the lock primitive — e.g. expected call frequency, number of concurrent acquirers, or GitHub remote rate-limit headroom. The `_cas_advance_with_retry` helper's retry bounds when applied to reconciler refs are also unspecified.
findings.location: What section / AC — general; '_cas_advance_with_retry' reuse

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require the new ADR to include an 'Alternatives considered' section that explicitly documents why ref→blob is preferred over ref→tiny-commit (and any other candidates), or promotes the ref→tiny-commit fallback to a first-class alternative with stated trade-offs — matching the project ADR standard (ADRs 0018–0023).
findings.finding: The plan commits to ref→blob over the well-established ref→tiny-commit alternative without documenting the justification, and the new ADR deliverable is not required to include an 'Alternatives considered' section. The project's own ADR template (ADRs 0018, 0019, 0020, 0021, 0023) consistently includes this section; the missing section means the novelty of the blob-object choice is not justified against the commit-object alternative in any durable artifact.
findings.location: What section ('Ref -> blob, so it never lives in the tickets working tree') and ADR deliverable requirement
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the ADR deliverable requirement: 'Include an Alternatives Considered section covering at minimum: (1) ref→tiny-commit (established, GC-safe, wider GitHub compat) vs. ref→blob (no commit overhead, smaller object); (2) file-based lock on a dedicated lock ref branch (no remote-GC risk) vs. bare ref lock.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'as needed' with a concrete decision: name the target module where `_cas_advance_with_retry` will be moved/imported from (e.g. `src/rebar/_engine/_git_cas.py`), the new symbol name if renamed, and update the AC to reference it explicitly.
findings.finding: 'Promote the reused private helper to a shared/semipublic symbol as needed' defers a concrete design decision (whether and how to expose `_cas_advance_with_retry`) to the executor's judgment at implementation time, with no guidance on what 'as needed' means or what the promotion interface should look like.
findings.location: What section, bullet 'Reuse the real CAS helper _cas_advance_with_retry'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to one generalization strategy for `_is_cas_mismatch` (signature extension vs. new variant), explicitly enumerate its existing call sites, and describe how backward compatibility for the `refs/heads/tickets` path is preserved or tested.
findings.finding: The `_is_cas_mismatch` function at `_advisory_lock.py:415` currently hard-codes `refs/heads/tickets`; the plan generalizes it but does not describe the existing callers' call sites, whether the signature change is backward-compatible, or a rollback path if the refactoring breaks the existing advisory-lock behavior.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Its discriminator `_is_cas_mismatch` (`_advisory_lock.py:415`, hard-coding `refs/heads/tickets` at line 425) is generalized to accept the ref name (or a ref-agnostic variant is extracted)'
findings.suggested_fix: Prefer extracting a ref-agnostic variant (e.g., `_is_cas_mismatch_for_ref(ref_name, ...)`) and keeping the existing `_is_cas_mismatch` as a thin wrapper calling it with `refs/heads/tickets`, so existing callers are unaffected and no rollback is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all callers of `_is_cas_mismatch` and state explicitly whether each is updated to pass the ref name or left using a backward-compatible default; document the chosen refactoring strategy (in-place generalization with default arg vs. extract-new-variant) in the plan or ADR.
findings.finding: The plan generalizes `_is_cas_mismatch` away from its hard-coded `refs/heads/tickets` string but does not enumerate or acknowledge existing callers that depend on the current hard-coded behavior, leaving open whether the change is safe for all existing callers.
findings.location: What section — '`_is_cas_mismatch` (`_advisory_lock.py:415`, hard-coding `refs/heads/tickets` at line 425) is **generalized to accept the ref name**'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: 'Existing callers: only `_cas_advance_with_retry` calls `_is_cas_mismatch`; it will be updated to pass the ref name as a parameter with a default of `refs/heads/tickets` to preserve existing behavior. No other callers exist.' If a new variant is extracted instead, state that the original function is left untouched.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate `test_advisory_lock_cas_race.py` and `test_advisory_lock_retry_jitter.py` as consumers of `_is_cas_mismatch`/`_cas_advance_with_retry` that must remain green after the generalization, and add a note in the plan confirming these tests cover the existing `refs/heads/tickets` path.
findings.finding: The existing tests `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_advisory_lock_cas_race.py` and `test_advisory_lock_retry_jitter.py` exercise `acquire_pass_lock` (and indirectly `_cas_advance_with_retry` / `_is_cas_mismatch`) against a `refs/heads/tickets` CAS race. The plan does not enumerate these test files as consumers that must be verified (or updated) after `_is_cas_mismatch` is generalized — if the generalization accidentally changes the discriminator logic for the tickets-ref path, these tests will silently start testing wrong behavior or break.
findings.location: `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/state/test_advisory_lock_cas_race.py`, `test_advisory_lock_retry_jitter.py` / plan section 'Reuse the real CAS helper'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the loose-blob GC window between `git hash-object -w` and `git update-ref`: either document that the window is safe because gc.auto never triggers within a single process's git subprocess chain, or add an explicit guard (e.g., write blob to a temp ref first, then rename to the lock ref, or use `git pack-objects` to make it permanent before update-ref).
findings.finding: Between `git hash-object -w --stdin` (blob written as a loose object, unreferenced) and `git update-ref <ref> <oid> 0000...` (ref created, blob now reachable), the blob exists in the object store with no ref pointing to it. If `git gc` runs in this window — possible on repositories with `gc.auto` enabled (the codebase's current posture since epic 97e7 explicitly *re-enabled* `gc.auto`) — the loose blob may be pruned before `update-ref` can reference it, causing `update-ref` to fail with an 'object does not exist' error rather than a CAS race error. The plan does not acknowledge this window or specify a guard.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Blob creation' section; AC line '`git hash-object -w` before `update-ref`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC0 to explicitly probe GC safety: after pushing the ref→blob, wait for a non-trivial interval (or trigger a synthetic GC via GitHub API) and verify the blob remains reachable, OR document in the ADR that GC survival is guaranteed by GitHub's ref-reachability semantics and cite the evidence.
findings.finding: AC0 probes push+fetch roundtrip of a ref→blob through the GitHub remote, but does not probe GC safety: whether GitHub's remote GC will collect the blob object between acquire (push) and release (fetch+delete) if the ref is the only thing keeping the blob reachable. Git locally guarantees reachability through refs, but GitHub's hosted-repo GC behavior for refs pointing at non-commit objects is undocumented and is a distinct, load-bearing capability assertion not covered by the roundtrip test.
findings.location: AC0 (Acceptance Criteria section) and What section ('AC0 feasibility spike')

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
